Each exercise in the book is illustrated with a picture that reveals the anatomy under the skin, including the main muscles at work and those that assist during the exercise.
The book includes step-by-step instructions on how to perfect exercise technique.
The book provides a list of advanced technical tricks to modify exercises for maximum effect.
The book teaches how to adjust grip, foot position, and body position.
The book teaches how to manipulate exercise trajectory and range of motion to emphasize different sections of the targeted muscle.
The book covers exercises using barbells, dumbbells, free weights, machines, wide grip, narrow grip, incline, decline, sitting, and standing positions.
You can review any exercise in the book in less than five minutes.
The book will help you make your workouts more precise and shift your training up a gear.
To change the way you look, you must modify your anatomy.
To change anatomy, you must first know anatomy.
Bodybuilding Anatomy is the ultimate reference, loaded with detailed technical discussions and illustrated with anatomical precision.
The book is systematically organized into muscle groups.
Each muscle group is subdivided into target zones.
Chapter 1 focuses on shoulders and how to develop boulder-like shoulders.
Chapter 1 reveals the secrets to a strong, injury-resistant rotator cuff.
Chapter 2 focuses on how to hammer and chisel the chest.
The shoulder is a ball-and-socket joint between the humerus bone of the upper arm and the scapula bone (shoulder blade).
Six main movements occur at the shoulder: flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, internal rotation, and external rotation.
During shoulder flexion, the upper arm is elevated forward toward the face.
During shoulder extension, the arm moves backward behind the plane of the body.
During abduction, the arm moves up and out to the side of the body.
During adduction, the arm is pulled in toward the side of the body.
Horizontal abduction and adduction occur when the arm moves in a horizontal plane at shoulder level, such as during chest flys or rear deltoid flys.
The deltoid muscle of the shoulder consists of three separate sections, or heads, each capable of moving the arm in different directions.
The anterior deltoid (in front) attaches to the clavicle and raises the arm forward (shoulder flexion).
The lateral deltoid (at the side) attaches to the acromion and lifts the arm out to the side (shoulder abduction).
The posterior deltoid (behind) attaches to the scapula and moves the arm backward (shoulder extension).
The rotator cuff is a group of four muscles that form a protective sleeve around the shoulder joint.
The rotator cuff is essential for shoulder stability and strength.
All four rotator cuff muscles originate from the scapula and pass across the shoulder joint to attach to the humerus bone.
The supraspinatus muscle lies above the shoulder joint and raises (abducts) the arm up and outward.
The infraspinatus and teres minor muscles are located behind the shoulder joint and rotate the arm outward.
The subscapularis muscle is situated in front of the shoulder joint and rotates the arm inward.
To perform a barbell shoulder press, sit on a bench with a shoulder-width grip on the bar with palms facing forward.
Lower the weight slowly in front until it touches the upper chest.
Push the weight vertically upward until the elbows lock out.
The primary muscle involved in the barbell shoulder press is the anterior deltoid.
Secondary muscles involved in the barbell shoulder press include the lateral deltoid, triceps brachii, trapezius, and upper pectoralis major.
A shoulder-width grip is preferred for the barbell shoulder press to target the anterior deltoid.
Wider grips minimize triceps contribution but increase the risk of shoulder injury.
A shorter repetition terminating the press just before lockout keeps tension on the deltoid by reducing triceps involvement during lockout.
Performing the barbell shoulder press while seated upright prevents cheating the weight upward using momentum from the legs.
The behind-the-neck press places the shoulder in more external rotation but increases the risk of shoulder injury.
The Front D E ltoi D Machine Shoulder Press targets the triceps brachii, upper pectoralis major, lateral deltoid, and anterior deltoid.
A neutral grip (palms facing each other) targets the anterior deltoid better than a pronated grip (palms facing forward) during the Front D E ltoi D Machine Shoulder Press.
A shorter repetition terminating the Front D E ltoi D Machine Shoulder Press just before lockout keeps tension on the deltoid.

The execution of the Front D E ltoi D Machine Shoulder Press involves sitting in the machine with your back straight, grabbing the handles, pushing vertically upward until your elbows lock out, and lowering the weight slowly back to shoulder level.
The Dumbbell Shoulder Press targets the triceps brachii, trapezius, upper pectoralis, lateral deltoid, and anterior deltoid.

The execution of the Dumbbell Shoulder Press involves sitting on a bench, holding a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder level, palms facing forward, pressing the dumbbells vertically upward until your elbows lock out, and lowering the dumbbells until they touch your shoulders.
Changing the orientation of the dumbbells affects hand position (grip).
Pressing the dumbbells up with palms facing forward (pronated grip) works both the anterior and lateral heads of the deltoid.
Pressing the dumbbells with palms facing together (neutral grip) makes the anterior deltoid work harder, minimizing involvement of the lateral head.
Holding the dumbbells with palms facing back (supinated grip) maximizes anterior deltoid effort.
Performing the exercise while seated upright is stricter than performing the exercise while standing because it prevents cheating the dumbbells upward using momentum generated by the legs.
This version uses three different hand positions during the repetition.
Begin the exercise by holding the dumbbells with your palms facing back (supination).
During the press, rotate the dumbbells so your palms face together (neutral grip) at the midpoint.
Finish the upward press with your palms facing forward (pronated grip) at lockout.
Perform the exercise by pressing one dumbbell at a time, alternating between the right arm and the left arm.
Sitting upright on the edge of an exercise bench, hold a dumbbell in each hand at arms’ length by your sides, thumbs pointing forward.
Lift one dumbbell out in front of your body and up toward shoulder level, keeping your elbow stiff.
Lower the weight to the starting position and repeat with the other dumbbell.
The primary muscle involved is the anterior deltoid.
Secondary muscles involved are the upper pectoralis and trapezius.
A neutral grip (palm inward, thumb pointing forward) emphasizes the anterior deltoid.
A pronated grip (palm down) allows the lateral deltoid to assist.
Begin with a neutral grip (thumb forward).
During the lift, rotate the dumbbell through 90 degrees so that your grip is pronated (palm down) at the top of the movement.
The barbell front raise targets the anterior deltoid, lateral deltoid, trapezius, and upper pectoralis major.
A narrow hand spacing during a barbell front raise emphasizes the anterior deltoid.
A wider grip during a barbell front raise requires assistance from the lateral deltoid.
The single dumbbell front raise targets the anterior deltoid with a neutral grip and narrow hand spacing.
The cable front raise targets the lateral deltoid, anterior deltoid, and trapezius.
The primary muscle involved in the short-bar attachment variation of the front deltoid exercise is the anterior deltoid.
The secondary muscles involved in the short-bar attachment variation of the front deltoid exercise are the lateral deltoid and trapezius.
The pronated overhand grip used in the short-bar attachment variation of the front deltoid exercise works the anterior and lateral heads of the deltoid.

To perform the short-bar attachment variation of the front deltoid exercise, face away from the machine with the cable running between your legs and grab the bar with both hands using a shoulder-width overhand grip.

To perform the rope attachment variation of the front deltoid exercise, face away from the machine with the cable running between your legs and grab the rope ends with both hands, thumbs pointing upward.
To perform the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise, stand upright and hold a dumbbell in each hand at arms’ length.
Raise your arms out to the sides in an arc until the dumbbells reach shoulder level during the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise.
Lower the dumbbells to your hips during the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise.
The primary muscle involved in the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise is the lateral deltoid.
The secondary muscles involved in the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise are the anterior deltoid, posterior deltoid, trapezius, and supraspinatus.
The lateral deltoid performs most of the work as the dumbbells are raised to shoulder level during the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise.
The trapezius takes over if the dumbbells are raised higher during the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise, so terminating the upward phase at shoulder level keeps tension on the deltoid.
Effort from the lateral deltoid is maximized when the dumbbells are held parallel to the floor during the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise.
Tilting the dumbbells with thumbs up externally rotates the shoulder and makes the anterior deltoid contribute to the motion during the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise.
Tilting the dumbbells with thumbs down internally rotates the shoulder, allowing the posterior deltoid to assist during the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise.
Lifting the dumbbells directly out to the sides hits the lateral deltoid during the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise.
Raising the dumbbells from in front of the hips with a forward arc makes the anterior deltoid assist during the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise.
If the arc of motion occurs behind the plane of the body, then the posterior deltoid contributes to the lift during the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise.

Because of the effect of gravity on the dumbbells, resistance is lower at the beginning of the movement and gradually increases to a maximum as the dumbbells are raised to shoulder level during the dumbbell standing lateral raise exercise.
To perform the one-arm dumbbell lateral raise variation, perform this exercise using one arm at a time, stabilizing your torso with your free hand on your hip.
The dumbbell seated lateral raise targets the lateral deltoid.
The dumbbell seated lateral raise also involves the anterior deltoid, posterior deltoid, trapezius, and supraspinatus.
Performing the dumbbell seated lateral raise seated on a flat exercise bench minimizes the use of momentum to swing the dumbbells up.
A vertical backrest can be used to support the torso and reduce stress across the lower back during the dumbbell seated lateral raise.
Terminating the upward phase of the dumbbell seated lateral raise at shoulder level keeps tension on the lateral deltoid.
Raising the dumbbells higher than shoulder level during the dumbbell seated lateral raise causes the trapezius to take over the work.
Holding the dumbbells parallel to the floor during the dumbbell seated lateral raise maximizes effort from the lateral deltoid.
Tilting the dumbbells with thumbs up during the dumbbell seated lateral raise externally rotates the shoulder and makes the anterior deltoid contribute to the motion.
Tilting the dumbbells with thumbs down during the dumbbell seated lateral raise internally rotates the shoulder, allowing the posterior deltoid to assist.

Resistance during the dumbbell seated lateral raise is lower at the beginning of the movement and gradually increases to a maximum as the dumbbells are raised to shoulder level due to the effect of gravity.
The cable lateral raise targets the trapezius, posterior deltoid, lateral deltoid, and supraspinatus.
During the cable lateral raise, stand side-on to the cable machine with one hand grasping the D-handle attached to a low pulley.
The primary muscle involved in the side deltoid is the lateral deltoid.
Secondary muscles involved in the side deltoid are the anterior deltoid, posterior deltoid, trapezius, and supraspinatus.
Terminating the upward phase of the side deltoid at shoulder height keeps tension on the lateral deltoid.
If the handle is raised higher than shoulder height during the side deltoid, the trapezius takes over the work.
The supraspinatus assists the lateral deltoid during the first 30 degrees of the side deltoid movement.
Starting a side deltoid repetition with your hand in front of the opposite thigh can increase the range of motion by extending the early phase of the movement.
The lateral deltoid is targeted best when the hand is raised directly out to the side during the side deltoid.
Performing the side deltoid raise in front of the plane of your body activates the anterior deltoid.
Raising your hand from the rear during the side deltoid activates the posterior deltoid.
Unlike dumbbell lateral raises, the cable pulley provides uniform resistance throughout the side deltoid motion.
To perform the machine lateral raise, sit on the machine with your elbows against the pads and grasp the handles.
Raise your elbows until your upper arms are at shoulder level and parallel to the floor during the machine lateral raise.
Lower your elbows to your sides during the machine lateral raise.
Machine raises provide a uniform resistance throughout the movement.
The supraspinatus assists at the start of the machine lateral raise, and the trapezius assists if the elbows are raised above shoulder level.
A pronated grip (palms down) internally rotates the shoulder and targets the lateral deltoid during the machine lateral raise.
A neutral grip (palms facing in) or supinated grip (palms up) externally rotates the shoulder and increases the contribution of the anterior deltoid during the machine lateral raise.
Changes in shoulder rotation are made easier by gripping the elbow pads and not holding the machine’s handles during the machine lateral raise.
Raising your elbows directly out to the sides during the machine lateral raise hits the lateral deltoid.
Performing the machine lateral raise with your elbows positioned forward on the pads makes the anterior deltoid assist.
The one-arm machine lateral raise improves focus and isolation.
Some machines are designed so that you face inward, stabilizing your torso against a chest pad during the one-arm machine lateral raise.
The Barbell Upright Row targets the lateral deltoid, teres minor, supraspinatus, and infraspinatus.
A typical starting position for the Barbell Upright Row involves holding a barbell at arms’ length using an overhand shoulder-width grip.
To execute the Barbell Upright Row, pull the bar vertically upward, raising your elbows to shoulder height.
Lower the bar slowly down to the starting position with the arms extended.
The primary muscles involved in the Barbell Upright Row are the lateral deltoid and trapezius.
Secondary muscles involved in the Barbell Upright Row include the anterior deltoid, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor.
A wider grip on the bar during the Barbell Upright Row helps target the deltoid.
A narrower grip on the bar during the Barbell Upright Row emphasizes the trapezius.
Lifting the barbell close to the body during the Barbell Upright Row targets the lateral deltoid.
Raising the bar through a forward arc away from the body during the Barbell Upright Row requires assistance from the anterior deltoid.
If the elbows are raised above shoulder level during the Barbell Upright Row, the trapezius takes over the work.
The Cable Upright Row targets the lateral deltoid and trapezius.
To execute the Cable Upright Row, grab a straight bar attached to the low pulley of a cable machine and hold the bar at arms’ length using an overhand shoulder-width grip.
Pull the bar vertically upward, raising your elbows to shoulder height during the Cable Upright Row.
Lower the bar slowly to the starting position with the arms extended during the Cable Upright Row.
The primary muscles involved in the side deltoid exercise are the lateral deltoid and trapezius.
Secondary muscles involved in the side deltoid exercise are the anterior deltoid and supraspinatus.
A wider grip on the bar during the side deltoid exercise targets the deltoid, while a narrower grip emphasizes the trapezius.
If the elbows are raised above shoulder level during the side deltoid exercise, the trapezius takes over the work.
With a torso upright during the side deltoid exercise, effort is focused on the lateral deltoid.
If the torso is tilted forward during the side deltoid exercise, the posterior deltoid assists in the movement.
The machine upright row variation of the side deltoid exercise uses a Smith machine that provides a single plane of vertical motion.
To perform the dumbbell bent-over raise, hold a dumbbell in each hand at arms’ length, bend forward at the waist, keeping your back straight and head up.
With palms facing each other, raise the dumbbells to ear level, keeping your elbows slightly bent during the dumbbell bent-over raise.
Lower the dumbbells to the starting position after completing the dumbbell bent-over raise.
The primary muscle involved in the rear delt exercise is the posterior deltoid.
Secondary muscles involved in the rear delt exercise are the lateral deltoid, trapezius, rhomboids, infraspinatus, teres minor, and teres major.
Holding dumbbells using a neutral grip (thumbs pointing forward) during the rear delt exercise allows the lateral deltoid to work.

Holding dumbbells using a pronated grip (thumbs pointing inward) during the rear delt exercise targets the posterior deltoid because the shoulder is rotated internally and the action of the lateral deltoid is reduced.
Because of the effect of gravity on the dumbbells, the resistance is lower at the beginning of the rear delt movement and gradually increases to a maximum as the dumbbells are raised.
With your torso flat and parallel to the floor during the rear delt exercise, emphasis is placed on the posterior deltoid.
If your torso is inclined with your chest upright during the rear delt exercise, the lateral deltoid contributes to the movement.
To perform the head-supported dumbbell raise, stand behind and in line with an incline exercise bench.

Bend forward at the waist until your head touches the top of the backrest, which should be set at a height that allows your torso to be almost parallel to the floor during the head-supported dumbbell raise.
Supporting your head restricts movement in the spine and prevents using momentum to swing the dumbbells up during the head-supported dumbbell raise.
The dumbbell seated bent-over raise targets the posterior deltoid.
The dumbbell seated bent-over raise involves the lateral deltoid, trapezius, rhomboids, infraspinatus, teres minor, and teres major.
A pronated grip on the dumbbells during the dumbbell seated bent-over raise targets the posterior deltoid.
A neutral grip on the dumbbells during the dumbbell seated bent-over raise allows the lateral deltoid to work.
The resistance in the dumbbell seated bent-over raise is lower at the beginning of the movement and increases as the dumbbells are raised.
With a flat torso during the dumbbell seated bent-over raise, emphasis is placed on the posterior deltoid.
With an inclined torso during the dumbbell seated bent-over raise, the lateral deltoid contributes to the movement.
The cable bent-over raise targets the posterior deltoid.
The cable bent-over raise involves the lateral deltoid, trapezius, rhomboids, infraspinatus, teres minor, and teres major.
To target the posterior deltoid, your arms should move directly out to the sides.
If your hands are raised in a forward arc in front of your head, the trapezius and lateral deltoid contribute to the exercise.
The posterior deltoid is better isolated when your torso is parallel to the floor, not when it is inclined with your chest and head up.
The range of motion at the start is increased if the hands are allowed to cross over (uncrossing the cables) as the handles are lowered.
The added distance and further stretch make the posterior deltoid work harder.
Unlike dumbbell raises, where the resistance varies during the lift, the cable pulley affords a uniform resistance throughout the motion.
The cable handle does not allow you to make changes in hand position or grip.
Do the One-Arm Cable Bent-Over Raise exercise using one arm at a time.
This unilateral version makes it possible to raise your hand higher and get a longer stretch at the bottom, thereby generating more work for the posterior deltoid.
Stabilize your torso by resting your free hand on your thigh.
Stand upright in the middle of a cable machine, facing the pulleys.
Using a thumbs-up grip, grab the handles attached to two high pulleys.
Hold the left-side handle in your right hand and the right-side handle in your left hand.
Pull your hands back and slightly down in an arc, arms nearly parallel to the floor, until your hands are in line with your shoulders, forming a T.
Return the handles to the starting position so that your right hand is directly in front of your left shoulder and your left hand is directly in front of your right shoulder.
To target the posterior deltoid, your arms should move directly back and slightly down, almost parallel to the floor.
If the hands are raised through a higher arc to a point above shoulder level, the trapezius and lateral deltoid make a bigger contribution to the movement.
The posterior deltoid is best targeted when your torso is upright, not when it is leaning too far forward or back.
Crossing your hands over one another at the starting position increases the range of motion and muscle stretch, thereby making the posterior deltoid work harder.

You can do the Supported Cable Reverse Crossover exercise sitting or standing either with the chest supported on the back of an incline exercise bench or with the chest against the pad of a preacher bench.
Position the bench centrally between two cable pulleys.
Whether you sit on the bench or stand over it, you must be positioned high enough to allow your arms to perform the exercise without obstruction.
The pulleys should be level with or just higher than your head.
This variation is easier on your lower back, so you can focus on working the deltoid.
The rear deltoid fly machine targets the posterior deltoid, trapezius, rhomboids, lateral deltoid, infraspinatus, teres minor, and teres major.
The rear deltoid fly machine provides a uniform resistance throughout the range of motion.
Using the horizontal handles with a pronated grip is the best method of isolating the posterior deltoid because the shoulder is internally rotated.
Using the vertical handles with a neutral grip allows the lateral deltoid to participate because the shoulder is externally rotated.
The posterior deltoid is worked best when the handles are grasped at or just below shoulder level, with your arms roughly parallel to the floor.
If the handles are grasped above shoulder level, then the trapezius performs a greater portion of the work during the exercise.
You can increase the range of motion by performing the exercise with one arm at a time.
Performing the exercise with one arm at a time reduces the relative contribution from the trapezius and the scapular retractor muscles, which thereby aids posterior deltoid isolation.
You can modify the range of motion during the one-arm version by changing your seated position on the machine.
Stand side-on to a cable pulley that is adjusted to waist height.
Grasp the handle with your outside hand, thumb pointing up.
Holding your elbow firmly against your waist, move the handle in an outward arc away from your body, keeping your forearm parallel to the floor.
Slowly return the handle to the starting position in front of your navel.
The primary muscles involved in the exercise are the infraspinatus and teres minor.
The secondary muscle involved is the posterior deltoid.
During the exercise, external rotation occurs at the shoulder joint from the combined action of the infraspinatus and the teres minor.
The hand moves in a horizontal arc with the forearm parallel to the floor.
The upper arm is vertical and the elbow is tight against the side.
The hand moves through an arc of approximately 90 degrees.
You cannot perform this exercise with a dumbbell while standing upright because gravity does not provide resistance for the rotator cuff.
To use a dumbbell, you will need to lie flat or recline so that gravity acts in the plane of rotator cuff function.
To perform the dumbbell external rotation variation, lie across a flat exercise bench, resting on your upper back with your elbows in contact with the bench.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand, position your forearm vertically upward, elbow bent at 90 degrees.
Keeping your elbow in contact with the bench, lower the dumbbell in a forward arc toward your waist until your forearm is roughly parallel to the floor.
To perform the reclining dumbbell external rotation variation, lie side-down on the floor or a flat exercise bench while holding a dumbbell in your upper hand.
The body position is similar to that described for the incline side raise later in the chapter.
To perform the exercise, stand side-on to a cable pulley that is adjusted to waist height.
Grasp the handle with your inside hand, thumb pointing up.
Holding your elbow firmly against your waist, pull the handle inward across the front of your body, keeping your forearm parallel to the floor.
Slowly return the handle to the starting position.
The primary muscle involved is the subscapularis.
The secondary muscle involved is the pectoralis major.
During the movement, the action of the subscapularis causes internal rotation at the shoulder joint.
The hand moves through a horizontal arc across the front of the torso, and the forearm remains parallel to the floor.
The elbow and upper arm are held tight against the side of the body.
The hand moves through a 90-degree arc.
You cannot perform this exercise with a dumbbell while standing upright because gravity does not provide resistance for the rotator cuff.
To use a dumbbell, you will need to lie flat so that gravity acts in the plane of rotator cuff function.
To perform the dumbbell internal rotation variation, lie across a flat exercise bench, resting on your upper back with your elbows in contact with the bench.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand, position your forearm out to the side, almost parallel to the floor.
Keeping your elbow bent at 90 degrees and in contact with the bench, raise the dumbbell in a forward arc until your forearm is vertical.
Execution step 1: Lie sideways on a bench with your torso inclined 45 degrees, supported by your underside arm. Hold a dumbbell with an overhand grip in your other hand.
Execution step 2: Raise the dumbbell to head height, keeping your elbow stiff.
Execution step 3: Lower the weight to waist level.
Muscles involved: Primary: Supraspinatus, Secondary: Lateral deltoid, anterior deltoid
Anatomic Focus Range: The supraspinatus initiates the arm raise, acting as the primary muscle during the first 15 to 20 degrees of abduction.
Anatomic Focus Resistance: Gravity on the dumbbell in the inclined position causes resistance to be highest during the early phase of the raise, focusing effort on the supraspinatus.
Anatomic Focus Trajectory: The supraspinatus is best isolated when the dumbbell is raised from in front of the hips.
Anatomic Focus Grip: A pronated grip (palm down) works best.

Cable Lateral Raise Variation: The cable lateral raise described earlier in this chapter is a good variation of the incline side raise. The supraspinatus initiates the arm raise and is active during the first 60 degrees of the movement. To focus on the rotator cuff muscle, terminate the upward phase when your hand reaches chest level.

Dumbbell Lateral Raise Variation: The dumbbell lateral raise (either sitting or standing) described earlier in this chapter is a good variation of the incline side raise. You can use one arm at a time or work both simultaneously.
The pectoralis major is a fan-shaped muscle that has two anatomic sections, or heads.
The upper clavicular head of the pectoralis major arises from the clavicle (collarbone).
The lower sternal head of the pectoralis major arises from the sternum (breastbone).
The two heads of the pectoralis major pass outward across the chest wall and merge into a single tendon that attaches to the humerus bone in the upper arm.
When the pectoralis muscle contracts, movement takes place at the shoulder joint.
The pectoralis major adducts, flexes, and internally rotates the arm.
The pectoralis major moves the arm forward and across the chest during movements such as a push-up or a bear hug.
The pectoralis major may be considered as having three sections—upper, middle, and lower—depending on the angle through which the arm is moved.
As the position of the shoulder joint changes, certain fibers of the chest muscle have a better mechanical advantage for creating motion.
Other fibers of the chest muscle are still active but are not able to contract as much because of the shoulder position.
The side wall of the chest is formed by the serratus anterior muscle.
The serratus anterior muscle arises from behind the scapula and passes forward around the chest wall to attach to the upper eight ribs.
The serrated edge of the serratus anterior muscle emerges from beneath the outer margin of the pectoralis muscle.
The serratus anterior muscle pulls (protracts) the scapula forward, stabilizing it against the rib cage.
The serratus anterior muscle is active during most chest exercises and works especially hard during the lockout phase of a push-up or bench press.
The pectoralis minor muscle lies deep beneath the pectoralis major muscle.
The pectoralis minor muscle has only a minor function and does not contribute to the size of the chest.
To execute a barbell incline press, sit on an incline bench with a shoulder-width overhand grip on a barbell.
Lower the weight slowly until the bar touches your upper chest.
Push the bar straight up until your elbows lock out.
The upper chest variation targets the upper pectoralis major.
Secondary muscles involved in the upper chest variation include the anterior deltoid and triceps brachii.
The angle of incline determines the trajectory of the upper chest exercise.
An incline of 30 to 45 degrees targets the upper pectoral muscle.
An incline of 60 degrees or more shifts the focus to the anterior deltoid.
A shoulder-width or slightly wider grip targets all areas of the upper pectoral muscle.
A narrow grip emphasizes the inner central portion of the chest and requires more effort from the triceps.
A wider grip provides a greater stretch, targeting the outer portion of the muscle, and minimizes triceps contribution.
As hand spacing increases, the risk of injury also increases.
To maximize pectoral work, flare your elbows out wide as the barbell is lowered.
A shorter repetition terminating the press just before lockout keeps tension on the pectorals and reduces triceps assistance.
The machine incline press provides better stability and safety than the standard barbell press.
A neutral grip (thumbs up, palms facing each other) emphasizes the pectorals better than a pronated grip (palms forward).

To perform the dumbbell incline press, sit on an incline bench, hold a dumbbell in each hand at chest level, palms facing forward, press the dumbbells vertically upward until your elbows lock out, and lower the dumbbells to your upper chest.
The dumbbell incline press targets the upper pectoralis major.
Secondary muscles involved in the dumbbell incline press include the anterior deltoid and triceps brachii.
The angle of incline determines the trajectory of the dumbbell incline press.
An incline of 30 to 45 degrees targets the upper pectoral muscle.
An incline of 60 degrees or more shifts the focus to the anterior deltoid.
A pronated grip affords a greater stretch as the weights are lowered to the starting position.
A neutral grip generates a better contraction at the lockout position.
To maximize pectoral work, flare your elbows out wide as the dumbbells are lowered and touch the dumbbells together at the top of the movement.
A shorter repetition terminating the press just before lockout keeps tension on the pectorals.
The lower the dumbbells descend, the more the chest muscle stretches.
Lowering the dumbbells too far can cause shoulder injury.
It is safer to terminate the descent when the dumbbells reach chest level.
The variable-grip dumbbell press begins with a pronated grip and rotates the dumbbells during the press so that the palms face together at lockout.
The dumbbell incline fly targets the upper pectoralis major.
Secondary muscles involved in the dumbbell incline fly include the anterior deltoid.
The angle of incline determines the trajectory of the dumbbell incline fly.
The upper pectoral is best targeted when the backrest is inclined 30 to 45 degrees to the floor.
The dumbbell incline fly works best when the dumbbells are held with a neutral grip.
A pronated grip can be used as a variation for the dumbbell incline fly.
The lower the dumbbells descend during the dumbbell incline fly, the greater the pectoral stretch.
It is safer to terminate the descent of the dumbbells during the dumbbell incline fly when they reach chest level.
Performing the machine fly with the seat low and the handles at eye level targets the upper pectorals.
The cable low-pulley fly is a variation of the dumbbell incline fly.
With each hand, grasp a D-handle attached to the low pulleys on a cable machine.
Stand upright midway between the weight stacks, facing forward.
Raise your arms in a forward arc until the handles meet at head height.
Keeping your elbows stiff, lower the handles back to the starting position.
The primary muscle involved in this exercise is the upper pectoralis major.
The secondary muscles involved are the anterior deltoid.
Standing forward so that the pulleys are slightly behind you affords a better trajectory to target the pectoral muscles.
Lying on an incline bench positioned centrally between the pulleys, grasp the D-handles attached to the low pulleys of a cable machine.
Handles should be level with your chest.
Raise your arms in an upward arc until the handles meet above your head.

To maximize pectoral isolation during the fly exercise, keep your elbows high (shoulder level) during the movement.

When the seat is high and the handles are held low, the lower chest is emphasized during the fly exercise.
In order to target the middle chest during the fly exercise, position the seat so the machine’s handles are level with your chest.

Do the one-arm machine fly exercise using one arm at a time.

Steeper declines shift the focus from the chest to the triceps for the barbell decline press.
Flare your elbows out as the bar is lowered to maximize pectoral isolation for the barbell decline press.
The ideal hand spacing for the barbell decline press is shoulder width.

A narrow (close) grip targets the inner pectorals and requires more work from the triceps for the barbell decline press.
A shorter repetition terminating the press just before lockout keeps tension on the pectorals and reduces the amount of triceps assistance for the barbell decline press.

The dumbbell decline press targets the lower pectoralis major (sternal head).
Secondary muscles involved in the dumbbell decline press include the anterior deltoid and triceps brachii.
A decline angle of 20 to 40 degrees to the floor best targets the lower pectoral muscle.
Dumbbells should move vertically up and down from the middle chest (nipple area) during the dumbbell decline press.
To maximize pectoral isolation during the dumbbell decline press, flare your elbows out wide during the descent and touch the dumbbells together at lockout.
A shorter repetition terminating the dumbbell decline press just before lockout keeps tension on the pectorals and reduces triceps assistance.
The lower the dumbbells descend during the dumbbell decline press, the more the chest muscle stretches.
It is safer to terminate the descent of the dumbbells during the dumbbell decline press when they reach chest level.
The variable-grip dumbbell decline press involves holding dumbbells with a pronated grip at the start and rotating them so that palms face each other (neutral grip) at lockout.
The dumbbell decline fly targets the lower pectoralis major (sternal head), anterior deltoid, and triceps brachii.
The primary muscle involved in the decline dumbbell fly is the lower pectoralis major (sternal head).
Secondary muscles involved in the decline dumbbell fly include the anterior deltoid and triceps brachii.
The decline angle in the decline dumbbell fly determines the trajectory of the exercise.
A decline angle of 20 to 40 degrees to the floor best targets the lower pectoral muscle.
The dumbbell orientation in the decline dumbbell fly affects hand position.
The decline dumbbell fly works best with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).

To perform the cable crossover, stand upright and grasp the D-handles attached to the high pulleys of a cable machine.
In the cable crossover, squeeze the handles down and together until your hands touch in front of your waist, keeping your elbows slightly bent.
Slowly return to the starting position in the cable crossover.
The primary muscle involved in the cable crossover is the lower pectoralis major (sternal head).
Secondary muscles involved in the cable crossover include the anterior deltoid and triceps brachii.
In the cable crossover, your torso should be upright or tilted forward slightly at the waist.
A low trajectory in the cable crossover, in which the handles meet in front of your hips or waist, targets the lowest fibers of the pectoral muscle.
A high trajectory in the cable crossover, in which the handles meet at chest level, targets the midsection of the pectorals.
Crossing your hands at the bottom of the movement in the cable crossover increases the range of motion and targets the inner central portion of the pectorals.

The chest dip targets the lower pectoralis major (sternal head), triceps brachii, and anterior deltoid.
A slight forward tilt during a chest dip targets the pectorals more.
A more upright posture during a chest dip shifts the focus to the triceps.

A standard grip on the parallel bars with thumbs pointing forward is best for targeting the chest during a chest dip.
A reverse grip with thumbs pointing backward during a chest dip shifts the focus to the triceps.
Machine dips tend to target the triceps more than the chest because most machines restrict torso motion.
The upper back is made up of the trapezius muscle.
The upper trapezius elevates the scapula to shrug the shoulders and rotates the scapula to assist shoulder abduction.
The middle trapezius retracts the scapula, pulling the shoulders backward.
The lower trapezius depresses the scapula downward.
The levator scapulae, rhomboid major, and rhomboid minor anchor the scapula to the spine.
The levator scapulae assists the upper trapezius to elevate the scapula.
The rhomboid major and rhomboid minor work with the middle trapezius to retract the scapula.
The middle back consists of the latissimus dorsi muscle.
The latissimus dorsi arises from the lower half of the spinal column and the rear ridge of the pelvic bone (posterior iliac crest).
The latissimus dorsi converges into a band-like tendon that attaches to the upper humerus, next to the tendon of the pectoralis major.
The latissimus dorsi pulls the upper arm downward and backward (shoulder extension).
The latissimus dorsi also pulls the arm in against the side of the body (adduction).
The lower back is made up of the erector spinae (or sacrospinalis) muscles that run the entire length of the spinal column.
In the lumbar region, the erector spinae splits into three columns: the iliocostalis, longissimus, and spinalis.
The erector spinae muscles stabilize the spine and extend the torso, arching the spine backward.
The trapezius and latissimus dorsi muscles are primarily concerned with movements of the shoulder and arm.
The sacrospinalis muscles cause movements of the spine and torso.
Exercises that target back muscles include shrugs, pull-downs, pull-ups, rows, and lumbar extensions.
The deadlift is a compound, multijoint exercise that involves all of the back muscles.
To perform a barbell shrug, hold a barbell at arms’ length in front of your thighs using an overhand shoulder-width grip.
Keeping your arms stiff, shrug your shoulders as high as possible, pulling the bar vertically upward.
Lower the bar slowly down to the starting position, stretching the trapezius.

Performing the rear shrug with the barbell behind the hips causes scapular retraction, pulling the shoulders backward and emphasizing the middle fibers of the trapezius.
Performing the machine shrug on a machine allows for a choice of grips: pronated (thumbs pointing in) or neutral (thumbs pointing forward).
A neutral grip during the machine shrug emphasizes the upper trapezius in the neck.
A pronated grip during the machine shrug targets the middle trapezius in the back.
Stand upright with a dumbbell in each hand, hands hanging at your sides for the dumbbell shrug.
Keeping your arms straight, shrug your shoulders upward as high as possible during the dumbbell shrug.
Lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position after the dumbbell shrug.
A neutral grip during the dumbbell shrug emphasizes the upper trapezius in the neck.
A pronated grip during the dumbbell shrug targets the middle trapezius in the back.
Tilting your torso slightly back at the waist during the dumbbell shrug targets the upper trapezius.
Leaning slightly forward during the dumbbell shrug targets the muscle lower down the neck.
Performing the dumbbell shrug while standing vertically upright hits the upper and middle sections of the trapezius.
The higher the weight is raised during the dumbbell shrug, the harder the trapezius works.
The farther the dumbbells are lowered during the dumbbell shrug, the greater the stretch at the bottom of the movement.
Hold the dumbbells in front of your body using a pronated grip for the retracting shrug.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together during the retracting shrug, finishing with the dumbbells at your sides in a neutral grip.
During the retracting shrug, the dumbbells are lifted upward (scapular elevation), working the upper trapezius, and backward (scapular retraction), working the middle trapezius.
The barbell upright row targets the trapezius, deltoid, levator scapulae, erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis), and forearms (wrist extensors, finger extensors).
A shoulder-width or narrower grip on the bar emphasizes the trapezius.
A wider grip works the deltoid.
Performing the row while standing vertically upright hits the trapezius centrally.
Tilting your torso slightly back at the waist targets the upper trapezius.
Leaning slightly forward targets the muscle lower down the neck.
To emphasize the trapezius (not the deltoid), raise the bar close to your body during the exercise.
The higher the bar is raised, the harder the trapezius works.
Raising the bar higher also increases the risk of shoulder-impingement pain.
The machine upright row targets the supraspinatus, levator scapulae, erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis), trapezius, and posterior deltoid.
The primary muscles involved in the upright row are the trapezius and posterior deltoid.
Secondary muscles involved in the upright row include the levator scapulae, erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis), supraspinatus, and forearms (wrist extensors, finger extensors).
Using a Smith machine for the upright row provides a single plane of vertical motion.
A shoulder-width or narrower grip on the bar during the upright row emphasizes the trapezius.
A wider grip on the bar during the upright row works the deltoid.
Performing the upright row while standing vertically upright hits the trapezius centrally.
Tilting your torso slightly back at the waist during the upright row targets the upper trapezius.
Leaning slightly forward during the upright row targets the muscle lower down the neck.
The higher the bar is raised during the upright row, the harder the trapezius works.
Raising the bar higher during the upright row increases the risk of shoulder-impingement pain.
The primary muscles involved in the cable seated row are the middle and lower trapezius and latissimus dorsi.
Secondary muscles involved in the cable seated row include the rhomboid major, rhomboid minor, and posterior deltoid.
Spacing your hands farther apart during the cable seated row targets the outer trapezius.
Placing your hands closer together during the cable seated row focuses on the inner portion of the trapezius.

The angled ends of a wide-grip pull-down bar offer advantages over a straight bar, including improved trajectory, less stress through the wrist joint, and a few extra inches of motion before the bar touches the chest.
Pulling the bar down behind the neck in a pull-down exercise is a less favorable trajectory that can cause shoulder joint injury.

Secondary muscles involved in the close-grip pull-down include the biceps brachii, lower trapezius, and posterior deltoid.
The primary muscle involved in the exercise is the inner section of the latissimus dorsi.

This exercise uses shoulder extension rather than adduction.

Take an overhand grip on the pull-up bar with hands 6 inches (15 cm) wider than shoulder width and arms extended.
Pull your torso upward until your chin touches the bar.
Lower your torso slowly down to the starting position.
The primary muscle involved in the wide-grip pull-up is the outer section of the latissimus dorsi.
Secondary muscles involved in the wide-grip pull-up include the posterior deltoid, lower trapezius, and rhomboid major.
Pull-ups are similar to pull-downs except that the resistance provided by your own body weight is not easily adjusted.

An underhand (supinated) grip on the bar facilitates a closer hand spacing, emphasizing shoulder extension rather than adduction.
As the hand spacing gets narrower, the focus shifts to target the inner lower section of the latissimus dorsi.

The handlebar attachment on some equipment allows a neutral grip with palms facing each other.

The barbell row targets the latissimus dorsi, erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis), trapezius, rhomboid major, rhomboid minor, and posterior deltoid.

An underhand grip on the barbell during a row facilitates a closer hand spacing, emphasizing shoulder extension and targeting the central inner section of the latissimus dorsi.

Pulling the barbell upward higher toward the chest during a row targets the upper latissimus dorsi and trapezius.
Pulling the barbell through a lower trajectory to touch the abdomen during a row targets the lower latissimus dorsi.

The T-bar row variation requires less effort to stabilize body position because one end of the bar pivots at a fixed point on the floor.
In the T-bar row, stand facing the loaded end with feet positioned on either side of the bar, keeping the spine straight and knees slightly bent, and lift the loaded end using the T-bar attachment.
Some T-bar row apparatuses feature an inclined chest pad that supports the torso and minimizes load across the lower spine.
The dumbbell row targets the latissimus dorsi, trapezius, rhomboid major, biceps brachii, and middle back.

A typical dumbbell row execution involves grasping a dumbbell in one hand with the palm facing in, resting the opposite hand and knee on a bench while keeping the spine straight and just above parallel to the floor, pulling the dumbbell vertically upward alongside the torso, raising the elbow as high as possible, and lowering the dumbbell down to the starting position.

A neutral grip with the dumbbell parallel to the torso works best for the one-arm dumbbell row.

Raising the dumbbell through a lower trajectory toward the abdomen targets the lower latissimus dorsi.
Maximize the range of motion by stretching the latissimus dorsi at the bottom position and raising the elbow as high as possible at the top of the movement.
Supporting your torso on the bench reduces stress through the spine.
Grab the handle of a low pulley with one hand using a neutral (thumb-up) grip for the one-arm cable seated row.
Pull the handle high toward your chest, keeping your spine straight for the one-arm cable seated row.
Return the handle to the starting position with the arm extended for the one-arm cable seated row.

Grab the handles of the machine with your arms extended in front of your body for the machine row.
Support your torso against the chest pad if the machine has one for the machine row.
Pull the handles toward your upper abdomen, keeping your spine straight for the machine row.
Return the weight to the starting position for the machine row.
Spacing your hands farther apart targets the outer latissimus dorsi.
Spacing your hands closer together isolates the inner latissimus dorsi.
A pronated (overhand) grip tends to target the upper and outer latissimus dorsi.
A neutral (thumbs up) grip hits the central section of the back.
A supinated (underhand) grip works the lower latissimus dorsi.

A lower trajectory toward the abdomen targets the lower latissimus dorsi.
Adjust the seat height to change trajectory.
Raising the seat creates a low trajectory; lowering the seat provides a high trajectory.
Pull your elbows back as far as possible and squeeze your shoulder blades together to maximize muscle contraction.
Load across the spine is reduced when the torso is supported against a chest pad.
The lumbar extension exercise involves lying facedown with hips supported on a bench and ankles secured under pads.
Begin the lumbar extension with your torso hanging down, bent 90 degrees at the waist.
Raise your upper body during the lumbar extension until your torso is just above parallel to the floor.
Primary muscles involved in the lumbar extension are the erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis).
Secondary muscles involved in the lumbar extension are the latissimus dorsi, gluteus maximus, and hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris).
Hand position for the lumbar extension can be interlocked behind the lower back or folded across the chest.
Resistance can be added to the lumbar extension by holding a weight plate against the front of the chest.
The lumbar extension movement should occur through an arc of about 90 degrees.
Avoid hyperextending your spine during the lumbar extension.
The erector spinae muscles stabilize and straighten the spine during the lumbar extension.
The gluteus maximus and hamstrings generate hip extension during the lumbar extension.
The incline lumbar extension is easier because the hips are supported high and the ankles are closer to the floor.
The incline lumbar extension shifts the focus away from the lumbar muscles onto the buttocks and hamstrings.
The machine lumbar extension is performed while seated on a machine that provides variable resistance.
Avoid flexing the spine too far forward or extending too far backward during the machine lumbar extension.
The deadlift involves taking a shoulder-width overhand grip on a barbell with arms extended.
Squat down, bending the knees and hips during the deadlift.
Keep your spine straight and elbows stiff while standing upright and lifting the bar upward to hip level during the deadlift.
Slowly lower the bar back to the floor by bending at the knees and hips during the deadlift.
The primary muscles involved in the barbell deadlift are the erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis), gluteus maximus, and hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris).

Secondary muscles involved in the barbell deadlift include the trapezius, latissimus dorsi, quadriceps (rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius), and forearms (wrist flexors, finger flexors).
For the barbell deadlift, hands should be spaced shoulder-width apart so that the arms hang vertically and the hands pass along the outer thighs.
An over-under grip with one palm facing forward and the other facing back is recommended for the barbell deadlift to prevent the bar from rolling.
Feet should be positioned directly below the hips with toes pointing straight ahead for the barbell deadlift.
The barbell should travel straight up and down and stay close to the body during the barbell deadlift.
The range of motion for the barbell deadlift is from the floor to the tops of the thighs, keeping the arms extended and elbows stiff.
During the barbell deadlift, the erector spinae muscles stabilize and straighten the spine while the gluteus maximus and hamstrings generate hip extension.
The spine should be kept straight throughout the barbell deadlift movement.
The barbell stiff-legged deadlift shifts the focus from the lower back to the buttocks and hamstrings.
The sumo-style deadlift, performed with a stance wider than hip-width, emphasizes the thigh muscles.
For the machine deadlift, stand upright and hold the bars of a Smith machine at arms' length using an overhand shoulder-width grip.
Bend forward at the waist and lower the bar downward, keeping the spine straight and elbows stiff during the machine deadlift.
Lift the bar back up to the starting position to complete the machine deadlift.
A Smith machine provides a single plane of vertical motion that can help focus effort during the machine deadlift.
An overhand grip is used for the machine deadlift to maintain the Smith machine bar in the unlocked position.
A slight bend in the knees helps with execution during the machine deadlift.
The range of motion for the machine deadlift is from hip level to down beyond the knees and back up again, keeping the arms extended and elbows stiff.
During the machine deadlift, the erector spinae muscles stabilize and straighten the spine while the gluteus maximus and hamstrings generate hip extension.
The cable pull-through is a variation of the deadlift performed facing away from a low pulley using a short bar with the cable passing between the legs.
The Good Morning exercise targets the lower back.
The Good Morning exercise involves bending forward at the waist until the torso is just above parallel to the floor.
The Good Morning exercise requires keeping the spine straight and knees stiff (straight or slightly bent).
The primary muscles involved in the Good Morning exercise are the erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis).
Secondary muscles involved in the Good Morning exercise include the latissimus dorsi, gluteus maximus, and hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris).
The grip for the Good Morning exercise should be slightly wider than shoulder-width apart using an overhand grip.
A slight bend in the knees helps with the execution of the Good Morning exercise.
The range of motion for the Good Morning exercise is about 90 degrees.
The erector spinae muscles stabilize and straighten the spine during the Good Morning exercise.
The gluteus maximus and hamstrings generate hip extension during the Good Morning exercise.
A variation of the Good Morning exercise is the machine lift, which is performed while seated using a pad across the upper back to provide resistance.
The human arm is divided into the upper arm and lower arm (forearm).
The upper arm consists of one bone, the humerus, while the forearm consists of two bones, the radius (located on the thumb side) and ulna (located on the little-finger side).
The elbow is a hinge joint formed at the junction of the humerus, radius, and ulna.
Two movements occur at the elbow joint: flexion and extension.
During elbow flexion, the forearm moves toward the upper arm.
During elbow extension, the forearm moves away from the upper arm.
Movement also takes place in the forearm when the radius rotates around the ulna.
Supination (palm up) and pronation (palm down) take place between the radioulnar joints.
The wrist joint is the junction between the lower end of the forearm bones and the small bones in the hand.
The biceps brachii muscle has two heads: the short head attaches to the coracoid process, and the long head arises from above the glenoid of the shoulder joint.
The biceps brachii muscle passes down alongside the humerus and attaches about 1.5 inches (4 cm) below the elbow joint onto a tuberosity on the inside of the radius bone.
The biceps brachii causes flexion at the elbow joint, such as when raising the hand toward the face.
The biceps brachii also causes supination of the forearm, such as when rotating the hand so that the palm faces up.
Two other muscles that flex (bend) the elbow are the brachialis and brachioradialis.
The brachialis muscle lies deep beneath the biceps brachii, arising from the lower half of the humerus and attaching to the ulna bone just below the elbow joint.
The brachialis lifts the ulna at the same time that the biceps brachii lifts the radius.
The brachioradialis muscle arises from the outer aspect of the lower end of the humerus and then travels down the forearm to attach to the radius just above the wrist joint.

The triceps brachii muscle has three heads: the long head arises from beneath the glenoid fossa of the shoulder joint, the lateral (outer) head arises from the outer surface of the humerus, and the medial (inner) head arises from the medial and rear surfaces of the humerus.
All three heads of the triceps brachii fuse at their lower ends to form a single tendon that attaches behind the elbow joint onto the olecranon process of the ulna bone.
The triceps brachii causes extension at the elbow, such as when straightening the arm.
The forearm is a mass of 20 muscles.
The forearm has two separate muscle compartments: the flexor group on the palm side and the extensor group on the reverse side.
The fleshy muscle portions of almost all forearm muscles are located in the upper two-thirds of the forearm.
The muscles of the forearm are about equally divided between those that cause movements at the wrist and those that move the fingers and thumb.
The wrist flexors and extensors are superficial (under the skin), whereas the finger flexors and extensors are deep (close to the bone).
The superficial group of wrist flexors and extensors cross both the wrist and elbow joints, so therefore have a greater stretch when wrist curls are performed with the elbow joint straight.
The wrist flexors are the palmaris longus, flexor carpi radialis, and flexor carpi ulnaris.
The wrist extensors are extensor carpi radialis longus, extensor carpi radialis brevis, and extensor carpi ulnaris.
The finger flexors are the flexor digitorum superficialis, flexor digitorum profundus, and flexor pollicis longus.
The finger extensors are the extensor digitorum, extensor pollicis longus, extensor pollicis brevis, and extensor indicis.
Supination, or rotating the hand so the palm faces up, is performed by the supinator and biceps brachii muscles.
Pronation, or rotating the hand so the palm faces down, is performed by the pronator teres and pronator quadratus.
A wide grip during a biceps curl focuses effort on the inner biceps brachii (short head).
A narrow grip during a biceps curl works the outer biceps brachii (long head).
With a straight bar, the underhand grip is fixed in supination (palms facing up) during a biceps curl.
The grip may be adjusted using an EZ bar during a biceps curl.
The bar should move up and down in an arc close to the body during a biceps curl.
To isolate the biceps brachii during a biceps curl, motion should occur at the elbows and not the shoulders.
Stopping a few degrees short of full elbow extension keeps tension on the biceps brachii as the barbell is lowered during a biceps curl.
Stand upright with the spine straight during a biceps curl.
Tilting the torso during a biceps curl cheats the bar upward with momentum.
Leaning slightly forward during a biceps curl makes the initial phase of the curl easier.
Leaning slightly backward during a biceps curl helps complete the final phase of the repetition.
Performing a curl with an EZ bar changes the grip from fully supinated (palms facing up) to a less supinated, nearly neutral (palms facing in) grip.
The EZ bar grip emphasizes the outer (long) head of the biceps brachii, the brachialis, and is less strenuous on the wrist joints.
To perform a dumbbell curl, sit on the edge of a weight bench with both feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at arms’ length by your sides, thumbs pointing forward, to begin a dumbbell curl.
Curl one dumbbell up toward your shoulder, rotating your hand so your palm faces upward, during a dumbbell curl.
Lower the dumbbell back down to the starting position and repeat with the opposite arm during a dumbbell curl.
The primary muscle involved in a dumbbell curl is the biceps brachii.
Secondary muscles involved in a dumbbell curl include the brachialis, brachioradialis, anterior deltoid, and forearm muscles (wrist flexors, finger flexors).
The dumbbell curl works the biceps brachii in two ways: elbow flexion and forearm supination.
To maximize contraction of the biceps brachii during a dumbbell curl, supinate the hand (palm facing up) as the dumbbell is raised.
Slide your palm over so your thumb rests against the inside of the plate when performing a dumbbell curl to increase the load on the biceps brachii during supination.
Position your torso upright with your spine straight during a dumbbell curl.
Tilting the torso during a dumbbell curl cheats the weight upward with momentum.
Leaning slightly forward during a dumbbell curl makes the initial phase of the curl easier.
Leaning slightly backward during a dumbbell curl helps complete the final phase of the repetition.
Use a full range of motion at the elbow during a dumbbell curl.
The dumbbell standing curl can be performed in a standing position, but this requires muscular effort in the legs.
The seated version of the dumbbell curl affords better focus.
Performing the dumbbell incline curl while seated on an incline bench helps focus effort on the lower portion of the biceps brachii, near the elbow.
The Concentration Curl is an exercise for the biceps brachii.

To perform the Concentration Curl, sit on the edge of a bench and hold a dumbbell in one hand at arm’s length, supporting your arm against the inside of your thigh. Place your free hand on your other thigh.
Curl the dumbbell up toward your shoulder by bending at the elbow.
Lower the dumbbell back down to the starting position.
The primary muscle involved in the Concentration Curl is the biceps brachii.
Secondary muscles involved in the Concentration Curl include the brachialis, brachioradialis, forearm muscles (wrist flexors, finger flexors).
An underhand grip maximizes contraction of the biceps brachii.
When the arm is vertical (shoulder directly above the elbow), resistance increases as the dumbbell is raised and effort is focused on the upper biceps brachii (peak).
When the arm is at an inclined angle (elbow in front of the shoulder), resistance is maximal at the start, so effort targets the lower section of the biceps brachii at the elbow.
Resting the upper arm against the thigh prevents movement at the shoulder and isolates the biceps brachii.
The torso should remain motionless during the Concentration Curl.
The Cable Curl is an exercise for the biceps brachii.
To perform the Cable Curl, face the weight stack and grasp the short bar attached to a low pulley using an underhand grip with arms straight.
Curl the bar up toward your shoulders by bending at the elbows.
Lower the bar back down to the starting position.
The primary muscle involved in the Cable Curl is the biceps brachii.
Secondary muscles involved in the Cable Curl include the brachialis, brachioradialis, anterior deltoid, forearm muscles (wrist flexors, finger flexors).
A grip that is wider than shoulder width during a biceps exercise focuses effort on the inner biceps brachii (short head).
A narrow grip during a biceps exercise works the outer biceps brachii (long head).
With a straight bar, the underhand grip is fixed in supination (palms facing up) during a biceps exercise.
Using an EZ bar during a biceps exercise switches the grip from the fully supinated position to a less supinated, nearly neutral (palms facing in) grip.
The EZ bar grip during a biceps exercise tends to focus effort on the outer (long) head of the biceps brachii and the brachialis muscle.
The EZ bar grip during a biceps exercise is less strenuous on the wrist joints.
Stand upright with a straight spine during a biceps exercise.
Fixing your elbows against your sides during a biceps exercise prevents movement at the shoulder and isolates the biceps brachii.

To perform a high-pulley curl, stand midway between the pulleys on a cable machine, grasp the D-handles attached to two high pulleys using an underhand grip, and hold your arms at shoulder level.
During a high-pulley curl, curl the handles toward your head.
The high-pulley curl emphasizes the long head of the biceps brachii and works the biceps brachii peak.
To perform a one-arm cable curl, use a D-handle attached to the low pulley of a cable machine and perform the exercise with one arm at a time.

To perform a barbell preacher curl, sit with your upper arms resting on the preacher bench, take a shoulder-width underhand grip on the bar with your arms out straight, curl the bar up toward your shoulders, and lower the weight back down to the starting position.
The primary muscle involved in a biceps exercise is the biceps brachii.
Secondary muscles involved in a biceps exercise include the brachialis, brachioradialis, and forearm muscles (wrist flexors, finger flexors).
A wide grip during a biceps exercise focuses effort on the inner biceps brachii (short head).
A narrow grip during a biceps exercise works the outer biceps brachii (long head).
With a straight bar, the underhand grip is fixed in supination (palms facing up) during a biceps exercise.
When the upper arms are supported at an inclined angle during a biceps exercise, resistance is maximal at the start, so effort is targeted on the lower section of the biceps brachii near the elbow.
Resting the upper arms on the bench during a biceps exercise prevents movement at the shoulders and helps isolate the biceps brachii.
Stopping a few degrees short of full elbow extension during a biceps exercise keeps tension on the biceps brachii as the barbell is lowered.
Adjust the seat height so that your armpit is snug against the upper edge of the pad during a biceps exercise.
The dumbbell preacher curl targets the biceps brachii, brachialis, brachioradialis, and forearm muscles (wrist flexors, finger flexors).

The dumbbell preacher curl is performed by sitting with your upper arm resting on a preacher bench, holding a dumbbell in one hand at arm’s length, curling the dumbbell up toward your shoulder by bending at the elbow, and lowering the dumbbell back down to the starting position.
Performing the dumbbell preacher curl one arm at a time with a dumbbell improves focus and isolation.
An underhand (palm facing up) grip during the dumbbell preacher curl maximizes contraction of the biceps brachii.

When the upper arm is supported at an inclined angle during the dumbbell preacher curl, resistance is maximal at the start, so effort is targeted on the lower section of the biceps brachii near the elbow.
Resting the upper arm on the bench during the dumbbell preacher curl prevents movement at the shoulder and thereby helps isolate the biceps brachii.
Stopping a few degrees short of full elbow extension during the dumbbell preacher curl keeps tension on the biceps brachii as the weight is lowered.
Adjust the seat height so that your armpit is snug against the upper edge of the pad during the dumbbell preacher curl.
The machine curl targets the biceps brachii, brachioradialis, and brachialis.

The machine curl is performed by grasping the bar using a shoulder-width underhand grip, with your elbows resting on the pad and your arms out straight, curling the bar toward your shoulders by bending at the elbow, and returning the bar to the starting position.
The biceps exercise primarily targets the biceps brachii muscle.
Secondary muscles involved in the biceps exercise include the brachialis, brachioradialis, and forearm muscles (wrist flexors and finger flexors).
A wide grip during a biceps exercise focuses effort on the inner biceps brachii (short head), while a narrow grip works the outer biceps brachii (long head).
An angled handlebar is less strenuous on the wrist joints than a straight bar during a biceps exercise.
An incline arm pad during a biceps exercise focuses effort on the lower portion of the biceps brachii.
The range of motion during a biceps exercise shifts focus from the lower biceps brachii in the initial phase to the middle biceps brachii (peak) as the weight is raised.
Unlike barbell or dumbbell curls, a machine curl provides uniform resistance throughout the movement.
A flat, horizontal arm pad during a biceps exercise focuses effort on the peak of the biceps brachii.
Performing a one-arm machine curl improves focus and isolation.
The triceps push-down exercise primarily targets the triceps brachii muscle.
Secondary muscles involved in the triceps push-down exercise include the deltoid and forearm muscles (wrist extensors).
A wide grip during a triceps push-down exercise focuses effort on the inner triceps brachii (long head), while a narrow grip focuses effort on the outer triceps brachii (lateral head).
A pronated (palms down) grip during a triceps push-down exercise emphasizes the outer lateral head of the triceps brachii, while a supinated (palms up) grip focuses effort on the inner long head.
A V-shaped bar during a triceps push-down exercise uses a neutral (thumbs up) grip that equally targets all three heads of the triceps brachii.
When the upper arms are perpendicular to the floor during a triceps push-down exercise, the outer triceps brachii (lateral head) contributes to the movement.
Performing a triceps push-down exercise with arms raised parallel to the floor focuses effort on the inner triceps brachii (long head).
Fixing the upper arms against your sides during a triceps push-down exercise prevents movement at the shoulders and isolates the triceps brachii.
Motion during a triceps push-down exercise should occur through the elbow only.
Unlike barbell or dumbbell exercises, a cable provides uniform resistance throughout the movement during a triceps push-down exercise.
Standing upright with a straight spine is the standard position for a triceps push-down exercise.
Leaning the torso slightly forward at the waist during a triceps push-down exercise provides better stability when using heavier weights.
The rope attachment during a rope push-down exercise affords a forcible pronation at the wrist, targeting the outer lateral head of the triceps brachii.
A reverse (underhand) grip during a reverse-grip push-down exercise focuses effort on the inner long head of the triceps brachii.
Performing a one-arm push-down exercise with a D-handle, using either an overhand or underhand grip, focuses effort and improves isolation.
The dip targets the triceps brachii.

A wide grip during a dip focuses effort on the inner triceps brachii (long head).
A narrow grip during a dip focuses effort on the outer triceps brachii (lateral head).
The standard grip (palms facing each other and thumbs forward) during a dip hits all three heads of the triceps brachii and emphasizes the inner long head.
Reversing the grip during a dip so that the palms face outward and the thumbs point back switches most of the effort to the outer triceps brachii (long head).
Keeping your elbows close to your sides during a dip helps isolate the triceps brachii.
Flaring your elbows out wide during a dip allows the chest muscles to assist.
To isolate the triceps brachii during a dip, keep motion at the shoulders to a minimum.
Movement during a dip should occur primarily at the elbows.

The tips mentioned for the dip also apply to the machine variation of the dip.
The lying triceps extension targets the triceps brachii.

In the lying triceps extension, hold a barbell or EZ bar at arms’ length above your chest with an overhand grip, hands spaced approximately 6 inches (15 cm) apart.
The triceps brachii muscle has three heads: the long head, the lateral head, and the medial head.
A wide grip during a triceps exercise emphasizes the inner triceps brachii (long head).
A narrow grip during a triceps exercise targets the outer triceps brachii (lateral head).
The vertical position of the arm during a triceps exercise stretches the inner (long) head of the triceps brachii.
Lowering the bar beyond the forehead toward the bench during a triceps exercise generates a greater stretch in the long head, favoring its contraction during the movement.
To isolate the triceps brachii during an exercise, motion should occur only at the elbows, not at the shoulders.
An overhand grip on a straight bar during a triceps exercise works the inner (long) head of the triceps brachii.
An underhand grip on a straight bar during a triceps exercise emphasizes the outer (lateral) head of the triceps brachii.
A neutral grip on an EZ bar during a triceps exercise works all three heads of the triceps brachii.
The barbell seated triceps press targets the triceps brachii muscle.
The barbell seated triceps press also involves the deltoid and forearm muscles (wrist flexors, wrist extensors).

The EZ-Bar Triceps Press offers a variety of grip choices.
The Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press targets the triceps brachii.
Secondary muscles involved in the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press include the deltoid and forearm (wrist flexors, wrist extensors).
The Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press can be performed with a neutral grip or a pronated grip.
A neutral grip on the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press works all three heads of the triceps brachii.
A pronated grip on the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press targets the inner (long) head of the triceps brachii.
The Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press requires a narrow grip on the dumbbell.
The elbows should flare outward during the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press.
The vertical position of the arm during the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press stretches the inner long head of the triceps brachii.
In order to isolate the triceps brachii during the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press, motion should occur only at the elbow joints.
The upper arms should remain vertical during the Dumbbell Seated Triceps Press.

The One-Arm Seated Triceps Press emphasizes the outer (lateral) head of the triceps brachii.
The One-Arm Seated Triceps Press is performed with one arm at a time while holding a dumbbell with the palm facing forward.
The Close-Grip Bench Press targets the triceps brachii and pectoralis major.
Secondary muscles involved in the Close-Grip Bench Press include the anterior deltoid.
The Close-Grip Bench Press is performed with a 6-inch (15 cm) overhand grip on the bar.
To target the triceps brachii, hand spacing should be narrower than shoulder width.
An underhand (supinated) grip on the bar also targets the triceps brachii, but this grip requires the hands to be spaced wide apart.
Keep your elbows close to your sides to emphasize the triceps brachii, not the chest.
A full range of motion (achieving full lockout) is required for maximizing triceps brachii effort.
Performing this exercise using an underhand (palms facing up) grip with your hands spaced more than shoulder-width apart also targets the triceps brachii.
Holding a dumbbell in one hand, bend forward at the waist and support your torso by resting your free hand on a bench or on your knee.
Begin with your upper arm parallel to the floor and your elbow bent 90 degrees.
Raise the dumbbell upward, straightening your arm until your elbow locks out.
The primary muscle involved is the triceps brachii.
Secondary muscles involved are the posterior deltoid and latissimus dorsi.
A neutral (thumb forward) grip works all sections of the triceps brachii.
Rotating the dumbbell so your palm faces up targets the outer (lateral) head.

Your torso should be slightly above parallel to the floor. If you stand too upright, you can’t perform the exercise effectively.
Perform this exercise while using a D-handle attached to a low pulley.
Unlike the dumbbell kickback, in which the resistance varies during the lift, the cable provides uniform resistance throughout the movement.
The wrist curl exercise targets the forearms.
To perform a wrist curl, sit on the edge of a bench, grasp a barbell with a shoulder-width underhand grip, and rest the backs of your forearms on your thighs.
Lower the bar by bending your wrists toward the floor during a wrist curl.
Curl the weight up by using wrist flexion during a wrist curl.
The primary muscles involved in the wrist curl are the palmaris longus, flexor carpi radialis, and flexor carpi ulnaris.
Secondary muscles involved in the wrist curl are the flexor digitorum superficialis, flexor digitorum profundus, and flexor pollicis longus.
The ideal hand spacing for a wrist curl is shoulder width or slightly narrower.
Your hands should be directly in line with your forearms during a wrist curl to minimize unnecessary stress in your wrist joints.
A supinated (palms facing up) grip is required for the wrist curl exercise.
You may place your thumbs either under or over the bar during a wrist curl, depending on personal preference.
A thumbless grip allows you to lower the bar farther during a wrist curl, increasing the range of motion.
Letting the bar roll down your fingers during the lowering phase of the repetition increases the range of motion during a wrist curl.
As the bar is curled up, the finger flexors work as you finger-curl the bar into your palm, and then the forearm flexors work as you curl your wrist up during a wrist curl.

Changing the position of your forearms in relation to the floor alters the resistance and adjusts the focus of the exercise during a wrist curl.
When your forearms are flat and parallel to the floor during a wrist curl, resistance is maximal at the beginning and decreases as the bar is lifted up.
When your forearms are angled to the floor so that your elbows are higher than your wrists during a wrist curl, resistance is minimal at the start and increases as the bar is curled up.
This trajectory is more effective at maximizing forearm contraction during a wrist curl.
You may support your forearms in several ways during a wrist curl: between your legs on a flat bench, on top of your thighs while seated on a bench, or on the incline pad of a preacher bench.
The dumbbell wrist curl is performed with one arm at a time while holding a dumbbell.
The preacher bench wrist curl is performed with your forearms resting on the incline pad of a preacher bench.
To perform a barbell standing rear wrist curl, stand upright, grasp a barbell at arms’ length behind your hips using a shoulder-width overhand grip.
Curl the bar by bending your wrists upward and backward during a barbell standing rear wrist curl.
Lower the weight toward the floor using wrist extension during a barbell standing rear wrist curl.
The primary muscles involved in the reverse wrist curl are the palmaris longus, flexor carpi radialis, and flexor carpi ulnaris.
