Hands should be directly in line with the forearms during the reverse wrist curl to minimize stress on the wrist joints.
The reverse wrist curl requires a pronated (overhand) grip.
The thumbs can be placed either under or over the bar during the reverse wrist curl.
Letting the bar roll down the fingers during the lowering phase of the repetition increases the range of motion in the reverse wrist curl.

As the bar is curled upward, the finger flexors work as you finger-curl the bar into your palm, and then the forearm flexors work as you curl your wrist up.
Because the finger flexors make up a significant portion of the forearm muscles, this extended repetition is more effective for building forearm mass.
The trajectory of the reverse wrist curl is vertical to the floor, with resistance being minimal at the start and increasing as the bar is curled up.

Slightly bending your knees eases the passage of the bar behind the thighs during the reverse wrist curl.
The primary muscles involved in the reverse wrist curl are the extensor carpi radialis longus, extensor carpi radialis brevis, and extensor carpi ulnaris.

The ideal hand spacing for the reverse wrist curl is shoulder width or narrower.
Hands should be directly in line with the forearms during the reverse wrist curl.
The reverse wrist curl requires a pronated (palms facing down) grip with thumbs around the bar.
Changing the position of your forearms in relation to the floor alters the resistance and adjusts the focus of the reverse wrist curl.
When your forearms are flat and parallel to the floor, resistance is maximal at the beginning and decreases as the bar is lifted up during the reverse wrist curl.
When your forearms are angled to the floor so that your elbows are higher than your wrists, resistance is minimal at the start and increases as the bar is curled up during the reverse wrist curl.

The leg is divided into the upper leg (thigh) and lower leg (calf).
The upper leg consists of one bone, the femur, whereas the lower leg consists of two bones, the tibia and the fibula.
The knee is a hinge joint formed at the junction between the femur and the tibia.
Two movements occur at the knee joint: flexion and extension.
During knee flexion, the lower leg bends toward the back of the thigh.
During knee extension, the lower leg moves away from the thigh so the leg becomes straight.
The hip is a ball-and-socket joint between the upper end of the femur and the pelvic bone.
Six main movements occur at the hip joint: flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, internal rotation, and external rotation.
During hip flexion the thigh bends up toward the abdomen, whereas during hip extension the thigh moves backward toward the buttocks.
The thighs separate during hip abduction and come together during hip adduction.
The ankle is a hinge-type joint between the lower tibia and fibula and the talus bone in the foot.
During ankle dorsiflexion, the toes lift off the floor and the foot moves toward the shin.
During ankle plantar flexion, the heel lifts off the floor and the foot moves away from the shin.
The quadriceps femoris, located in front of the thigh, has four separate heads: the rectus femoris, the vastus medialis, the vastus lateralis, and the vastus intermedius.

The abdominal wall can be divided into two anatomic parts, each of which functions differently.
The front wall consists of one muscle, the rectus abdominis (also known as the abs).
The rectus abdominis muscle arises from the lower margin of the rib cage and sternum and passes vertically downward to attach on the pubic bone.
The two rectus abdominis muscles (one on each side) are encased in a sheath of fascia that forms the central demarcation down the middle of the abs, known as the linea alba.
Fascia divisions in the muscles are responsible for the six-pack appearance.
The rectus abdominis muscles cause flexion of the trunk, bending the torso forward toward the legs.
The motion is carried out by the upper abs, which pull the rib cage down toward the pelvis, or by the lower abs, which lift the pelvis up toward the chest.
The side wall consists of three layers of muscles.
The external oblique is the outer visible layer that passes obliquely downward from the rib cage to the pelvic bone.
The internal oblique is the middle layer that passes obliquely upward from the pelvic bone to the ribs.
The internal oblique lies under the external oblique, and the fibers of the two muscles pass at right angles to one another.
The transversus abdominis is the innermost layer that lies horizontally across the abdominal wall.
Contraction of the oblique muscles on one side causes the torso to bend sideways.
Contraction of the obliques simultaneously on both sides assists the rectus muscle in flexing the trunk and splints the abdominal wall whenever a weight is lifted.
Only the outer external oblique is visible.
The serratus anterior muscle forms part of the side wall of the chest.
The serratus anterior muscle arises from the scapula behind and passes forward around the chest wall to attach to the upper eight ribs.
The serrated edge of the serratus anterior muscle emerges from beneath the outer margin of the pectoralis muscle, sending fingerlike projections into the external oblique.
The serratus anterior muscle pulls (or protracts) the scapula forward, stabilizing the scapula against the chest wall.
The serratus anterior muscle provides an essential accessory function whenever the pectoralis major and latissimus dorsi muscles contract.
The serratus anterior muscle can also be targeted during exercises that work the oblique muscles.

Elevating your legs increases the resistance of a crunch.
Your thighs should be positioned at a 90-degree angle to your torso during a crunch.
You may support your lower legs on a flat bench or position your feet on the floor close to your buttocks during a crunch.
The crunch motion occurs in the upper spine, and your shoulders rise a few inches off the floor.
Your lower back remains in contact with the floor during a crunch.
The crunch motion does not involve movement at the hips, unlike the sit-up.
You can increase the difficulty of a crunch by placing your hands behind your head or elevating your legs on a bench.
The Machine Rope Crunch targets the rectus abdominis, external oblique, and internal oblique.
The Machine Rope Crunch can be performed facing either toward or away from the weight stack.
The higher the hand position during the Machine Rope Crunch, the greater the difficulty.
The torso should move from the upright position to almost parallel to the floor during the Machine Rope Crunch.
The Machine Rope Crunch can be performed with a greater range of motion if you position yourself a short distance away from the pulley.
Resistance during the Machine Rope Crunch can be altered by adjusting the weight stack.
The Machine Crunch targets the rectus abdominis and external oblique.
To perform the Machine Crunch, sit in the seat of the machine, grasp the handles, and place your feet under the ankle pads.
The torso should move from the upright position to almost parallel to the floor during the Machine Crunch.
The Machine Crunch With Chest Pad targets the rectus abdominis, external oblique, and internal oblique.
The hand position during the Machine Crunch With Chest Pad depends on the machine's design.
The foot position during the Machine Crunch With Chest Pad depends on the machine's design.
The body position during the Machine Crunch With Chest Pad depends on the machine's design.
The torso should move from the upright position to almost parallel to the floor during the Machine Crunch With Chest Pad.
Resistance during the Machine Crunch With Chest Pad depends on the machine's design.
The Seated Sit-Up targets the rectus abdominis, external oblique, and internal oblique.
Secondary muscles involved in the Seated Sit-Up include the quadriceps (rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius), and hip flexors (sartorius, iliopsoas).
The starting position for the Seated Sit-Up is sitting on the apparatus with knees bent 90 degrees.
Secure your feet under a roller pad or comparable support during the Seated Sit-Up.
You may hold your hands together behind your lower back, cross them in front of your chest, or interlock them behind your head during the Seated Sit-Up.
The relative resistance in the Seated Sit-Up increases as your hands shift from your lower back to your chest to your head.
Your torso should be close to vertically upright in the starting position and should lower back approximately 60 to 90 degrees during the Seated Sit-Up.
Tension is released from the abdominal muscles when you lean too far back or bend too far forward during the Seated Sit-Up.
You can add resistance to the Seated Sit-Up by holding a weight plate to your chest.
The Twisting Seated Sit-Up involves adding a twisting motion during the sit-up.
During the Twisting Seated Sit-Up, as you sit up, twist your torso, directing your right elbow toward your left knee.
Lower your back to the starting position after twisting your torso during the Twisting Seated Sit-Up.
During the next repetition of the Twisting Seated Sit-Up, direct your left elbow toward your right knee.
The Incline Leg Raise targets the rectus femoris, lower rectus abdominis, and external oblique.
For the Incline Leg Raise, lie supine (face up) on an incline abdominal bench with your legs down, hands holding onto the bench above and behind your head.
The lower AbdominAls variation targets the lower rectus abdominis.
Secondary muscles involved in the lower AbdominAls variation include the external oblique, internal oblique, iliopsoas, and rectus femoris.
In the lower AbdominAls variation, your hands stabilize your torso by grasping the bench or handles above your head.
In the lower AbdominAls variation, keep your feet together and your knees slightly bent.
In the lower AbdominAls variation, your upper torso should remain in contact with the bench.
To maximize contraction in the lower abdominals during the lower AbdominAls variation, lift your pelvis off the bench slightly as you raise your legs.
To maximize muscle contraction on the way up during the lower AbdominAls variation, raise your knees as high as possible toward your chest.
To keep tension on the abs during the lower AbdominAls variation, do not lower your legs all the way down or allow your feet to touch the floor.
The angle of the bench to the floor affects the degree of difficulty in the lower AbdominAls variation.
Tilting the bench at a steeper angle makes the lower AbdominAls variation more difficult.
Reduce resistance in the lower AbdominAls variation by lowering the bench and decreasing the incline.
Increase resistance in the lower AbdominAls variation by raising the bench and increasing the incline.
For added resistance during the Dumbbell Incline Leg Raise, perform the exercise while holding a dumbbell between your feet.
To perform the Hanging Leg Raise, grasp a chin-up bar with your hands or place your elbows in a pair of ab slings.
Let your legs hang down during the Hanging Leg Raise.
Lift both knees, together and slightly bent, toward your chest during the Hanging Leg Raise.
Slowly lower your legs to the starting position without swinging during the Hanging Leg Raise.
The Hanging Leg Raise targets the rectus abdominis.
Secondary muscles involved in the Hanging Leg Raise include the external oblique, internal oblique, and iliopsoas.
For the Hanging Leg Raise, take a shoulder-width overhand grip on the chin-up bar and hang with your arms straight.
Alternatively, use a pair of supportive upper-arm sleeves, like the ab sling device, for the Hanging Leg Raise.
Keep your feet together and your knees slightly bent during the Hanging Leg Raise.
Your torso should hang vertically, perpendicular to the floor during the Hanging Leg Raise.
Raise your knees as high as possible to maximize muscular effort during the Hanging Leg Raise.
As you lower your legs during the Hanging Leg Raise, keep your knees slightly bent to maintain tension on the abdominals.
As you raise your legs during the Hanging Leg Raise, lift your pelvis to maximize contraction in the lower abdominals.
This exercise is harder if you try to keep your legs straight during the Hanging Leg Raise.
The more you bend your knees, the easier the Hanging Leg Raise becomes.
On the vertical leg raise apparatus, your back is supported against a backrest and your elbows rest on pads.
This prevents your legs and torso from swinging during the Vertical Leg Raise.
The Knee-Up exercise targets the lower abdominal muscles.
The primary muscles involved in the Knee-Up exercise are the rectus abdominis.
Secondary muscles involved in the Knee-Up exercise include the external oblique, internal oblique, iliopsoas, and rectus femoris.
To perform the Knee-Up exercise, sit on the edge of a flat, secure bench with legs hanging down and knees slightly bent.
Grip the bench behind you for support during the Knee-Up exercise.
Raise your knees toward your chest, keeping your legs together during the Knee-Up exercise.
Lower your legs until your heels almost touch the floor during the Knee-Up exercise.
The Reverse Crunch exercise targets the lower abdominal muscles.
The primary muscles involved in the Reverse Crunch exercise are the rectus abdominis.
Secondary muscles involved in the Reverse Crunch exercise include the rectus femoris and external oblique.
To perform the Reverse Crunch exercise, lie on a flat, secure bench with your feet positioned so that your knees and hips are bent 90 degrees.
Grasp the bench behind your head for support during the Reverse Crunch exercise.
Lift your pelvis off the bench until your feet point to the ceiling during the Reverse Crunch exercise.
Lower your legs to the starting position during the Reverse Crunch exercise.
The primary muscle involved in the variation is the rectus abdominis.
Secondary muscles involved in the variation include the external oblique, internal oblique, and hip flexors (iliopsoas, rectus femoris).
In the starting position for the variation, your thighs should be vertical and your lower legs should be parallel to the bench, resulting in a 90-degree bend at your knees and hips.
Keep your feet and legs together during the variation.
Keep your upper torso in contact with the bench during the variation.
To perform the variation, contract your lower abs to lift your pelvis off the bench, raising your legs until your toes point to the ceiling.
If you have difficulty balancing on a bench, you can perform the variation while lying on the floor.
On hip flexor machines, resistance is provided by the machine and transmitted via a strap across your lower thighs.
To perform the straight-leg sit-up, lie flat on the floor with your legs straight, feet secured, and arms at your sides.
Keeping your back straight, raise your torso off the floor until your hands touch your knees during the straight-leg sit-up.
Lower your torso to the starting position after raising it during the straight-leg sit-up.
The primary muscle involved in the straight-leg sit-up is the lower rectus abdominis.
Secondary muscles involved in the straight-leg sit-up include the external oblique, internal oblique, upper rectus abdominis, and hip flexors (sartorius, iliopsoas, rectus femoris).
To perform the straight-leg sit-up, raise your torso off of the floor, keeping your spine straight.
Your shoulders should rise about 6 to 12 inches (15-30 cm) off the floor or until your hands touch your knees during the straight-leg sit-up.
The motion occurs at the waist and hips during the straight-leg sit-up, in contrast to crunches, where movement occurs at the upper spine.
Keep your legs straight during the straight-leg sit-up.
The backs of your knees and your heels should be in contact with the floor throughout the movement during the straight-leg sit-up.
Secure your feet under a roller pad or comparable support during the straight-leg sit-up.
As an alternative, have a partner hold your ankles during the straight-leg sit-up.
Keeping your arms out straight, your hands should slide along your thighs during the straight-leg sit-up.
Terminating the motion when your hands touch your knees keeps focus on the lower abdominal muscles during the straight-leg sit-up.
You can increase the degree of difficulty by placing your hands behind your head or by holding a weight to your chest during the straight-leg sit-up.
The Twisting Sit-Up exercise involves sitting on a decline bench, hooking your feet under the pad, leaning back, and positioning your hands behind your head.
During a Twisting Sit-Up, as you sit up, twist your torso, directing your right elbow toward your left knee.
After lowering back down to the starting position, direct your left elbow toward your right knee during the next repetition of a Twisting Sit-Up.
The primary muscles involved in the Twisting Sit-Up are the rectus abdominis, external oblique, and internal oblique.
Secondary muscles involved in the Twisting Sit-Up are the serratus anterior, sartorius, iliopsoas, and rectus femoris.
For the Twisting Sit-Up, position your hands behind your head and secure your feet under a roller pad or comparable support.
Keep your knees bent during a Twisting Sit-Up to reduce stress on the lower back.
In the top position of a Twisting Sit-Up, your torso should be vertically upright with one elbow almost touching the opposite knee.
Lower your torso until almost parallel to the floor, about three-quarters of the way down during a Twisting Sit-Up.
Tilting the bench at a steeper angle makes the Twisting Sit-Up harder.
Increase resistance in the Twisting Sit-Up by tilting the bench at a steeper incline or holding a small weight plate behind your head.
The Broomstick Twist exercise involves sitting upright on the edge of a flat bench and holding a broomstick behind your neck.
Twist your upper body from side to side during the Broomstick Twist exercise.
When twisting to the right during the Broomstick Twist, feel the right oblique muscles contract.
The Oblique Crunch exercise involves lying on your left side with your knees bent and together and your right hand behind your head.
Slowly lift your upper body by contracting the obliques on your right side during the Oblique Crunch.
Lower your torso back down after lifting it during the Oblique Crunch.
The primary muscles involved in the Oblique Crunch are the external oblique, internal oblique, and rectus abdominis.
Secondary muscles involved in the Oblique Crunch are the serratus anterior.
The incline oblique crunch is performed using an incline abdominal chair.
To perform the incline oblique crunch, secure your feet on the support platform and lean back sideways into the seat, resting on one buttock only.
Place your uppermost hand behind your head and crunch your torso upward to perform the incline oblique crunch.
The machine oblique crunch is performed while sitting obliquly (on one buttock at a time) in the seat of a crunch machine, working one side at a time.
The cable oblique crunch is performed by grabbing a D-handle attached to the high pulley of a cable machine.
Crunch downward, directing your elbow toward the opposite knee during the cable oblique crunch.
Slowly return to the starting position after crunching downward during the cable oblique crunch.
The primary muscles involved in the cable oblique crunch are the external oblique, internal oblique, and serratus anterior.
The secondary muscle involved in the cable oblique crunch is the rectus abdominis.
You may grasp the handle above or alongside your head during the cable oblique crunch.
You may perform the cable oblique crunch while standing, kneeling, or sitting.
You may perform the cable oblique crunch facing either toward or away from the weight stack.
Your torso should move from the upright position to almost parallel to the floor during the cable oblique crunch.
You can alter the resistance of the cable oblique crunch by adjusting the weight stack.
To perform the cable standing oblique crunch, stand side-on to the weight stack and grab the D-handle attached to a high pulley with your nearside hand.
Crunch downward, directing your elbow to the hip on the same side during the cable standing oblique crunch.
The rope oblique crunch involves holding the rope attachment with both hands and crunching with a twist to one side and then the other.
The rope oblique crunch motion is similar to that used during twisting sit-ups.
The Dumbbell Side Bend exercise targets the external oblique, internal oblique, and serratus anterior muscles.
Secondary muscles involved in the Dumbbell Side Bend include the rectus abdominis and quadratus lumborum.
For the Dumbbell Side Bend, hold a dumbbell at arm’s length by your side in one hand and place the other hand behind your head.
Stand with your feet hip-width apart for the Dumbbell Side Bend.
When bending to the right side during the Dumbbell Side Bend, you work the left obliques, and vice versa.
Your torso should bend approximately 45 degrees or until the dumbbell becomes level with your knee during the Dumbbell Side Bend.
The torso should move directly sideways without leaning to the front or the back during the Dumbbell Side Bend.
Avoid using a heavy dumbbell for the Dumbbell Side Bend.
Large, overdeveloped oblique muscles will make your waist appear bulky.
The Cable Side Bend exercise targets the external oblique, internal oblique, serratus anterior, and rectus abdominis muscles.
The obliques muscles involved are the primary external oblique, internal oblique, and serratus anterior.
Secondary obliques muscles involved are the rectus abdominis and quadratus lumborum.
For the oblique exercise, hold the D-handle attached to the low pulley at arm’s length in one hand and place your other hand behind your head.
Stand with your feet slightly wider than your hips for the oblique exercise.
The position of your legs and arms should resemble a four-pointed star for the oblique exercise.
Stand sufficiently away from the pulley so that the gripping arm remains outstretched for the oblique exercise.
When you hold the weight in your right hand you work the left oblique muscles, and vice versa for the oblique exercise.
Your torso should bend sideways back and forth through approximately 60 degrees, or between 10 and 2 o’clock when looking in a mirror for the oblique exercise.
You may bend toward the weight stack to stretch the oblique before contracting the muscle in the opposite direction for the oblique exercise.
Your torso should move directly sideways without leaning forward or backward for the oblique exercise.
Adjust the resistance on the weight stack according to your ability for the oblique exercise.
Use caution with heavier weights, as overdeveloped oblique muscles will make your waist appear bulky for the oblique exercise.
For the dumbbell pullover, lie with your upper back resting across a flat bench.
Hold a dumbbell straight above your chest for the dumbbell pullover.
Lower the dumbbell down and back until it reaches bench level, inhaling deeply and stretching your rib cage as you do so for the dumbbell pullover.
Pull the weight back up to the vertical position, exhaling as you do so for the dumbbell pullover.
Primary muscles involved in the dumbbell pullover are the serratus anterior, intercostals, and latissimus dorsi.
Secondary muscles involved in the dumbbell pullover are the pectoralis major, pectoralis minor, and triceps brachii.
For the dumbbell pullover, grip the dumbbell by placing your palms against the inside of the weight plate at one end, making a diamond shape around the bar with your thumbs and index fingers.
Your torso should remain still and parallel to the floor, with your upper back resting on the bench and your feet firm on the floor for stability for the dumbbell pullover.
Move the dumbbell through an arc of about 90 degrees for the dumbbell pullover.
Aim for a full stretch in your rib cage as you lower the weight for the dumbbell pullover.
Do not use a heavy weight because the shoulder joint is vulnerable to injury during this exercise for the dumbbell pullover.

This exercise strengthens the serratus anterior but doesn’t directly target the obliques. However, it is included in this section because the serratus anterior is worked during most oblique exercises.
You can perform this exercise using a barbell or a machine.
